questions.question: A form uses placeholder text inside input fields instead of visible labels. What is the primary accessibility problem?

options.text: Placeholder text uses the wrong color
Placeholder text disappears when users start typing, leaving them without context
Placeholder text cannot be translated
Placeholder text is too small to read

feedback.a: Incorrect. While placeholder text often has low contrast, the primary problem is that it disappears when typing.
feedback.b: Correct! When placeholder text disappears, users lose the context of what information the field requires.
feedback.c: Incorrect. Placeholder text can be translated. The issue is it disappears when typing.
feedback.d: Incorrect. While placeholder text contrast can be an issue, this is not the primary problem.

questions.question: A designer creates an email input field that uses an envelope icon instead of a visible text label. What must the designer annotate for this field to be accessible?

options.text: The icon must be 24 by 24 pixels
No annotation is needed since the icon is universally understood
The icon color must have 4.5:1 contrast
An accessible name for the icon (e.g., "Email address")

feedback.a: Incorrect. The 24x24 pixel requirement applies to touch targets, not icons serving as labels.
feedback.b: Incorrect. No icon is universally understood, and screen readers cannot interpret visual symbols.
feedback.c: Incorrect. While the icon needs sufficient contrast, this doesn't solve the labeling problem for screen readers.
feedback.d: Correct! When an icon replaces a text label, the designer must annotate an accessible name.

questions.question: A user enters "john.smith@" in an email field and submits the form. Which error message best meets WCAG requirements?

options.text: Please enter a valid email address, for example: name@company.com
There was a problem with the information you entered
The email field contains an error that must be corrected
Email format is incorrect, please review your entry carefully

feedback.a: Correct! This message identifies the problem and suggests how to fix it. SC 3.3.1 requires errors be identified, and SC 3.3.3 requires suggestions.
feedback.b: Incorrect. This is too vague. It doesn't identify which field has the error.
feedback.c: Incorrect. This identifies there's an error but doesn't explain what's wrong or how to fix it.
feedback.d: Incorrect. This identifies the problem but 'review your entry carefully' isn't a helpful suggestion.

questions.question: A form field's border turns red when there's an error, but no error text is displayed. What WCAG failure does this represent?

options.text: Both SC 1.4.3 Text Contrast and SC 3.3.4 Error Prevention
Both SC 1.4.6 Contrast Enhanced and SC 3.3.3 Error Suggestion
Both SC 1.4.1 Use of Color and SC 3.3.1 Error Identification
Both SC 1.4.11 UI Contrast and SC 3.3.2 Labels and Instructions

feedback.a: Incorrect. SC 1.4.3 is about text contrast ratios. SC 3.3.4 applies to legal/financial submissions.
feedback.b: Incorrect. SC 1.4.6 is AAA-level contrast. SC 3.3.3 requires suggestions, but this scenario fails before that: there's no error text at all.
feedback.c: Correct! This fails SC 1.4.1 because color is the only indicator of the error. It also fails SC 3.3.1 because the error is not described in text.
feedback.d: Incorrect. SC 1.4.11 addresses UI contrast ratio, not whether color is used alone. SC 3.3.2 is about labels, not errors.

questions.question: Why does SC 1.3.5 (Identify Input Purpose) require autocomplete support for fields collecting personal information?

options.text: It prevents users from submitting forms with empty required fields
It helps users with motor and cognitive disabilities complete forms faster with fewer errors
It ensures form field borders have sufficient contrast
It requires all form fields to display inline validation messages

feedback.a: Incorrect. Required field validation relates to SC 3.3.1 and 3.3.2, not SC 1.3.5.
feedback.b: Correct! Autocomplete reduces keystrokes for users with motor disabilities and reduces memory demands for users with cognitive disabilities.
feedback.c: Incorrect. Form field contrast is covered by SC 1.4.11, not SC 1.3.5.
feedback.d: Incorrect. Validation messages relate to SC 3.3.1, not SC 1.3.5.
